introTxt: Almost 5 years building frontend and full-stack applications in React and Next.js — from e-commerce and SaaS to cultural platforms. Before code, I spent four years managing a landmark cultural venue in Kraków, running a team of ~100 across bars, restaurants, concerts, and festivals. That background gave me something most developers don't start with: a deep understanding of how products actually get used by real people under real pressure. Today I work as a freelancer and within agency teams, owning projects end-to-end — from architecture decisions to deployment.

sections.text: Projects built as part of the development team at Navarra Lab — a design studio and software house operating between Berlin and Kraków.
Projects I own end-to-end — from initial scope and architecture through to deployment and handoff.

cards.title: Background
cards.content: I studied Comparative Studies of Civilizations at Jagiellonian University — not a typical CS degree, but it taught me how to think across systems and contexts. Before writing my first line of production code, I spent four years as Venue Manager at Forum Przestrzenie Kraków — a landmark cultural space with bars, restaurants, a concert hall, and a rotating cast of festivals. Managing ~100 people, handling budgets, vendor negotiations, and event logistics built the same skills I use every day as a developer: leading under pressure, communicating across disciplines, and making decisions with incomplete information.

cards.title: Now
cards.content: I transitioned into frontend development in 2022, working at Navarra Lab across projects for cultural institutions, startups, and e-commerce brands in Berlin and Kraków. Since 2024 I also take on freelance work, delivering full-stack applications from scope to production. I gravitate toward complex interfaces, accessibility challenges, and modernizing legacy systems. I'm currently exploring AI technologies and building applications with integrated LLM agents.

cards.title: Off the Clock
cards.content: When I'm not coding, you'll find me behind the decks — DJing is how I stay connected to the music and events world I came from. For resetting, I chase wind and waves: kitesurfing and surfing are my way of switching off completely. Based between Kraków and Warsaw, bilingual in English and Polish.

cards.title: Accessibility
cards.content: Building for everyone isn't optional — it's the baseline. I implement WCAG-compliant interfaces because usability and inclusion are the same thing. Every project I touch gets proper semantic HTML, keyboard navigation, and screen reader support.

cards.title: Ownership
cards.content: I don't wait for tickets. Whether I'm embedded in an agency team or running a freelance project solo, I take responsibility for the full delivery — from architecture decisions to the last pixel in production.

cards.title: Pragmatism
cards.content: I pick tools that solve the problem, not tools that look impressive on a tech radar. Next.js, TypeScript, Tailwind, Payload, Shopify — whatever fits the constraint. No stack religion, no over-engineering.

paragraphs: I work best with founders, studios, and product teams that need someone who can move between product thinking and implementation without dropping either. Sometimes that means leading a build from scope to launch. Sometimes it means stepping into an existing codebase, finding the weak points, and making it calmer, faster, and easier to maintain.

My default mode is clarity. I like defining scope early, surfacing tradeoffs before they become problems, and shipping in small, defensible increments. I care about aesthetics, but not at the cost of usability. I care about speed, but not the fake kind that creates cleanup work three weeks later.
If you need a frontend or full-stack partner who can own delivery, communicate cleanly, and keep accessibility and polish in scope from day one, I'm open to selected collaborations.

sections.intro: Proposition one. An asymmetrical editorial grid with uneven card heights and a looser rhythm than the rest of the page.

notes.title: Speed Is Felt
notes.text: Interfaces are judged before they are understood. Perceived responsiveness is part of the design, not a technical afterthought.

notes.title: Clarity Wins
notes.text: If a feature needs a paragraph to justify itself, the UI probably still needs another pass.

notes.title: Real Users Break Nice Systems
notes.text: The structure only proves itself when content is messy, priorities shift, and people use the product in ways nobody planned.

notes.title: Leave Room
notes.text: Good systems are specific enough to guide the work and loose enough to survive iteration.

notes.title: Polish Matters
notes.text: Tiny choices around spacing, motion, and copy tone often decide whether a product feels considered or generic.

sections.intro: Proposition two. A more sequential, almost exhibition-like stack of statements with stronger vertical pacing.

notes.title: Start With Friction
notes.text: The best place to begin is usually not the homepage dream state. It is the moment where users slow down, hesitate, or get lost.

notes.title: Structure Reduces Noise
notes.text: The cleaner the hierarchy, the less explanation the interface has to do later.

notes.title: Aesthetic Decisions Carry Product Weight
notes.text: Visual choices are never neutral. They shape trust, pace, and how much attention a user is willing to spend.

notes.title: Momentum Needs Restraint
notes.text: Shipping fast matters, but only when the system stays understandable enough to keep moving.

notes.title: Good Products Breathe
notes.text: Negative space, sensible defaults, and calm transitions make complexity feel manageable.

sections.intro: Proposition three. A denser board of fragments, tags, and statements that feels more like a wall of working references.

notes.title: Readability First
notes.text: People should understand where they are and what matters before they admire the layout.

notes.title: Motion Needs Purpose
notes.text: Animation earns its place when it guides attention, reveals structure, or changes mood deliberately.

notes.title: Constraints Are Useful
notes.text: A limited system often produces a stronger result than endless freedom.

notes.title: Interfaces Remember Tone
notes.text: The product does not only say things through copy. It says them through spacing, timing, and visual restraint.

notes.title: Make The Important Thing Obvious
notes.text: Strong hierarchy is usually more persuasive than decorative complexity.

sections.intro: Proposition four. A timeline-like reading experience with alternating cards and a stronger sense of sequence.

notes.title: Observe
notes.text: Look for the actual pressure points first: navigation confusion, weak hierarchy, inconsistent states, or slow feedback.

notes.title: Reduce
notes.text: Cut the unnecessary branch before adding another component, state, or interaction.

notes.title: Shape
notes.text: Build a structure that can hold real content, not just ideal mockup conditions.

notes.title: Refine
notes.text: Tighten the details only after the hierarchy and behavior are already doing the right work.

notes.title: Ship
notes.text: A finished release should feel calm, legible, and ready for the next iteration rather than over-explained.

sections.intro: Proposition five. A numbered mosaic with larger visual weight and more graphic contrast than the other text sections.

notes.title: Design The Pace
notes.text: The rhythm of the page is as important as the content on it.

notes.title: Use Contrast Sparingly
notes.text: If every element shouts, nothing leads.

notes.title: Let Layout Carry Meaning
notes.text: A different structure should signal a different kind of information.

notes.title: Keep Systems Flexible
notes.text: Design should hold up when the perfect asset is missing or the content changes shape.

notes.title: Memorable Beats Busy
notes.text: A page needs one or two strong moves, not twenty decorative ones.
